references.citation: Mehta, R., Zhu, R. J., & Cheema, A. (2012). Is Noise Always Bad? Exploring the Effects of Ambient Noise on Creative Cognition. Journal of Consumer Research, 39(4), 784-799.
references.relevance: Foundational study proving moderate ambient noise (~70dB) enhances creativity through processing disfluency. Explains why coffee shops and noisy kitchens may boost creative thinking.

references.citation: Mednick, S. A. (1962). The associative basis of the creative process. Psychological Review, 69(3), 220-232.
references.relevance: Seminal theory that creativity stems from forming remote associations between unrelated concepts. Novel environments provide diverse stimuli that flatten associative hierarchies, enabling more creative leaps.

references.citation: Maddux, W. W., & Galinsky, A. D. (2009). Cultural borders and mental barriers: the relationship between living abroad and creativity. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 96(5), 1047-1061.
references.relevance: INSEAD/Columbia study showing that adapting to foreign cultures enhances creativity through psychological transformation and broadened perspectives. Direct parallel to micro-displacement.

references.citation: Moreau, C. P., & Dahl, D. W. (2005). Designing the Solution: The Impact of Constraints on Consumers' Creativity. Journal of Consumer Research, 32(1), 13-22.
references.relevance: Demonstrates how input constraints (limited resources) can enhance creative output by forcing cognitive restructuring and overcoming functional fixedness. Directly applicable to the 'crappy laptop setup' observation.

references.citation: Bloom, N., Han, R., & Liang, J. (2024). Hybrid Working from Home Improves Retention without Damaging Performance. Stanford Working Paper / Nature.
references.relevance: Stanford study showing hybrid work is 'win-win-win' for productivity, performance, and retention. Nuances the WFH vs office debate — displacement is not the same as remote work.

references.citation: University of Essex & University of Chicago (2024). Impact of Hybrid Work on Innovation.
references.relevance: Found that hybrid workers produced lower-quality innovative ideas compared to consistent office workers, but the mechanism was loss of spontaneous interaction — not displacement itself.
